segments.s129-g9: پس is a sentence-initial temporal/conclusive adverb ('then'). آگاهی is the noun subject 'information/news' placed before the verb (common in Persian). آمد is past-tense 3rd-person singular 'came'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from' and introduces هاماوران ('the level plains') as the source.
segments.s129-g10: بدشت is a prepositional/location phrase (ب + دشت) meaning 'to/on the plain' and precedes the following agent(s). سواران and نیزه‌وران are coordinated agent nouns ('horsemen' and 'spearmen'); the locative phrase sets the scene and the nouns name those who arrive or appear there.
segments.s129-g11: که here is the subordinating particle introducing an indirect question/relative content clause ('that/what'). رستم is the clause subject (proper name). The repeated به before مصر and بربر is the preposition marking the places/peoples ('in/to Egypt and in/to the Berbers'); چه is the interrogative 'what' used inside an indirect question and کرد is past 3rd-person singular 'did'.
segments.s129-g12: بران is an archaic/poetic prepositional/demonstrative element meaning roughly 'to/against those' and links to شهریاران ('those kings') as the addressees/recipients of the action. به‌روز is an adverbial/time expression 'on the day' (here 'on the day of battle') and نبرد is the noun 'battle' that specifies the day.
segments.s129-g13: دلیری is the noun 'bravery/valor' placed before the verb for emphasis. بجستند is past 3rd-person plural and here means 'they sought' or 'they displayed/searched for'. گرد is an adverb 'around/about' that modifies the following noun, and سوار is the agent noun 'horseman/rider'—together describing the movement or presence of mounted men.
segments.s129-g14: عنان پیچ is a compact phrase made of 'عنان' (reins) + 'پیچ' (twist) functioning as an epithet ('those who twist the reins' / 'rein‑twisters'). The coordinating و links a string of epithets: مردافگن ('man‑slayer' — an attributive epithet) and نیزه‌دار ('spear‑bearer'). This is a poetic list of warrior-types; adjectives/nouns follow one another in apposition.
segments.s129-g15: نوشتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they wrote' and takes نامه 'a letter' as its direct object. یکی here is an indefinite modifier meaning 'a certain'/'one' and precedes the adjective مردوار; the noun + modifier order (نامه یکی مردوار) is a poetic/postpositive pattern where the descriptive element follows the noun.
segments.s129-g16: سخنهای is the plural construct 'utterances/words' (literally 'words of...') and is modified by the following coordinated adjectives شایسته و آبدار. In poetry adjectives commonly follow the noun; شایسته means 'appropriate/worthy' and آبدار is used figuratively ('full‑bodied, rich/forceful') describing the letter's phrasing.
segments.s129-g57: تراگر is a conditional particle meaning 'if you' (ترا + گر). سزا بودی is a past/subjunctive condition 'were worthy/deserving' (سزا = 'desert/worthiness', بودی = 'were'). ایران بدان names what is deserved (ایران) with بدَان (ب + دان) functioning adverbially/relationally ('in/for that'), so the segment says 'if you were deserving of Iran...'.
segments.s129-g58: نیازت is 'your need' (نیاز + enclitic -ت). نبودی is the negative past/conditional 'would not have been' (here 'would not have been/you would not have had'). به مازندران marks the thing needed/target ('for/to Mazandaran'); together: 'your need would not have been for Mazandaran' = 'you would not have needed Mazandaran.'
segments.s129-g59: چنین گفت is an introductory phrase 'he said thus/so'. The following clause treats ایران as subject and دوه رویه (literally 'two‑faced') as predicate modifier; مراست is an archaic fused form (مرا است) meaning 'is to me' or 'is toward me', so the clause reads 'Iran is two‑faced toward me.' Word order is typical Persian: introductory verb phrase + subject + predicate.
segments.s129-g60: بباید is the modal 'one must/ought' and governs the verbal noun شنیدن 'to hear' (infinitival/masdar use). سخنهای راست is the object phrase 'true/honest words' with سخنهای pluralized and راست as the qualifying adjective placed after the noun. Together: 'one must hear honest words.'
segments.s129-g61: که here is the relative/connecting 'that/who'. پور فریدون is a name phrase 'son/descendant of Fereydun' (پور + name). نیای من‌ست is 'is my ancestor' (نیای = ancestor; من‌ست = مرا است/'belongs to me' or 'is mine' used here as copular predicate). The clause states the genealogical claim.
segments.s129-g62: همه modifies 'شهر ایران' to mean 'all the towns/cities of Iran'. سرای من‌ست (سرای = 'dwelling/abode' here) plus the copula من‌ست ('is mine') yields 'are my dwelling' or 'belong to me.' Word order places the possessed noun phrase before the predicate 'سرای من‌ست.'
segments.s129-g63: و دیگر is a connective 'and again/furthermore'. به بازوی شمشیرزن uses به instrumentally here ('by/with the arm of the swordsman'), so the phrase names the means ('by the swordsman's arm'). Word order places the instrument phrase before the verb that follows.
segments.s129-g64: تهی کردم is a causative/resultative construction 'I made (it) empty' or 'I emptied'. از تازیان انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'the assembly of the Arabs' (انجمن = assembly; از تازیان = 'of/from the Arabs'), so the whole clause means 'I emptied the assembly of the Arabs.'
segments.s129-g33: زمین کوه تا کوه is a poetic spatial phrase where زمین (= the land/country) is qualified by کوه تا کوه (“from mountain to mountain,” i.e. across the whole country). پرخون is an adjective ‘blood‑filled/bloodied’ and کنیم is the 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive form used here with a hortative/determined sense (‘we will/may we make’ or simply ‘we will make’). Together: “we will make the land, from mountain to mountain, bloodied.”
segments.s129-g34: ز here marks a genitive/agentive relation (‘of/from the enemy’), so ز دشمن بیابان = ‘the enemy’s plain/desert.’ چو = ‘like/as’ and introduces the comparison with جیحون (the Amu Darya). کنیم repeats the same verbal form as above (1st‑person plural), so the clause means ‘we will make the enemy’s plain like the Jihun.’
segments.s129-g35: فرستاده تازی is the object phrase ‘the Arab messenger/envoy.’ برافگند is a past tense verb (3rd‑person) meaning ‘threw off/dispensed with/dismissed.’ و رفت adds a second past action ‘and he went/left.’ The clause uses simple past narration.
segments.s129-g36: به marks direction (‘toward/into’), so به بربرستان = ‘toward Barbarestan.’ روی بنهاد is an idiom literally ‘he put (his) face (toward)’ = ‘he turned/steered his course to.’ بنهاد is 3rd‑person singular past. و + تفت links a further past action; تفت (here) is used as a past action meaning ‘scorched/burned/laid waste.’
segments.s129-g37: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when.’ نامه‌بر is the subject ‘the messenger/envoy.’ شاه ایران is the title + domain ‘the King of Iran.’ رسید is the 3rd‑person singular past ‘arrived/reached.’ The whole is a temporal subordinate clause: ‘When the messenger reached the Shah of Iran…’
segments.s129-g38: بران (literally ‘thereupon’/‘on that’) links to the previous clause. گونه گفتار = ‘such a kind of speech/words’ (گونه = manner/kind, گفتار = speech). بایسته is an adjectival predicate ‘proper/fitting.’ دید is past ‘he saw/found.’ The construction بایسته دید means ‘he judged it fitting’ or ‘he found such words to be proper.’
segments.s129-g39: از ایشان (written here as ازیشان) = ‘from them’ / ‘of them’ (i.e. their proposal). پسند آمدش is an impersonal/experiential idiom ‘it pleased him’ (پسند آمد = ‘was pleasing’); آمدش is ‘came to him.’ کارکرد here is used as ‘he carried it out / put it into action’ (literally ‘performed the work’). Together: ‘Their proposal pleased him, and he carried it out.’
segments.s129-g40: به + name marks the recipient: به افراسیاب = ‘to Afrasiab.’ آن زمان = ‘at that time’ (آن used as temporal ‘that/then’). نامه کرد is a light‑verb construction where نامه (letter) + کرد (did/made) = ‘he sent/wrote a letter.’ The clause states the action taken then: ‘he sent a letter to Afrasiab.’
segments.s129-g25: ازیشان (ازِ + ایشان) = “from/on their side”; poetic cliticization joins preposition and pronoun. The sequence ازیشان و از ما marks both parties (“of them and of us”). بسی is an adverb meaning “many/much” modifying کشته. کشته شد is a passive past construction (کشته + شد) meaning “were killed”; classical Persian often uses شد to form passives where Modern Persian uses شدند or آمد/شد.
segments.s129-g26: زمانه is the subject meaning “the times/fortune.” به here indicates direction or state (“toward/into”), followed by هر نیک و بد “every good and bad.” گشته شد is a two-part perfect/passive-like verbal phrase (perfect participle گشته + شد) meaning “became/turned” — a poetic way to say the times changed.
segments.s129-g27: کنون = “now.” کآمد is an archaic/past-form verb meaning “came/it came” and governs the following information; از کار او is a genitive phrase “of his affairs/deeds.” آگهی (literally “announcement/information”) is the thing that arrived — word order is inverted from Modern Persian (we would expect آگهی آمد).
segments.s129-g28: که introduces the reported content (“that …”). تازه شد is a past statement “was renewed/turned fresh” (تازه + شد). آن تخت شاهنشهی is a noun phrase “that throne of kingship/imperial throne,” with آن as demonstrative, تخت “throne,” and شاهنشهی qualifying it as royal/imperial.
segments.s129-g29: همه = “all/everyone.” نامداران is a plural adjective/noun meaning “the renowned/famous ones” and combines with شمشیرزن “swordsmen” to yield “all the famed swordsmen” (the phrase stacks modifiers/nouns as is common in older Persian).
segments.s129-g30: برین is scribal/poetic spelling for بر این (“upon/at this”). کینه گه is a compound (کینه + گه) meaning “place/occasion of hatred” (گه = place/occasion). بر شدند انجمن is the verbal core: بر شدند (“rose up / assembled” — شدند is plural past) and انجمن is the assembly; the order places the location phrase before the verb and the noun انجمن after, typical of epic diction.
segments.s129-g31: چو = “when/if.” شه = “the king.” برگراید is a present/future or gnomic form meaning “turns/leans toward” or “gives over” (بر + گراید). ز marks the agent/source “to/from,” so ز بربر عنان literally “the reins to the barbarian” — i.e., giving control to a barbarian. The clause is conditional/temporal.
segments.s129-g32: به گردن = “to the neck,” marking the target. برآریم is first-person plural hortative/jussive (“let us raise/let us bring” or here more forcefully “we will thrust/put”), built from بر + آریم (archaic). یکسر is an adverb “straight/entirely/at one stroke.” سنان (plural of سنان or collective) = “spear(s)” — together the phrase means “we will drive a spear clean into his neck.”
segments.s129-g105: بشد is the past/preterite of شدن here used impersonally/agentively meaning 'he became / he moved' (sense 1). تیز functions adverbially 'swift(ly)'. با is the preposition 'with' and لشکر سوریان is a genitive noun phrase 'the army/host of the Surians' (سوریان plural). The clause therefore says he advanced/moved quickly together with the Syrian host.
segments.s129-g106: بدان is a prepositional/relative cluster meaning 'to/with that, in regard to that' (ب + دان viewed here as 'with that in mind'); سود جستن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'seeking profit' (سود = 'benefit' as noun, جستن = infinitive 'to seek'). سرآمد زیان puts two nouns together where the copula is omitted: 'the result/end (سرآمد) [was] loss (زیان)' — سرآمد here means 'conclusion/result'.
segments.s129-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. روشن زمانه is a nominal phrase 'the time/situation being clear' (روشن = 'clear/evident', زمانه here as 'the state of affairs / fortune'). بران گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner / so' (بران = 'thereupon/in that regard', گونه = 'manner/way'). دید is the simple past 'he saw/beheld', so the whole clause is 'when he saw the situation thus'.
segments.s129-g108: ازانجا is an adverb 'from there/thereupon'. سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. شهر توران is the noun phrase 'the city of Turan'. کشید is the past of کشیدن here meaning 'he drew / moved / advanced' (a common poetic use with سوی to mean 'proceeded toward'). The clause describes physical movement away from the prior point.
segments.s129-g109: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic pronoun ش = his). خسته is the predicate adjective 'weary'. و links the next phrase. کشته لشکر reads 'the army(ies) slain' (کشته as 'slain' modifying لشکر). بهر here is a prepositional/relational word 'for / toward / with respect to' (in classical usage it can indicate purpose or relation); the full short clause states his heart's weariness and the slaughter of the host(s). Note the phrase is compact/poetic and omits auxiliary verbs.
segments.s129-g110: همی is the customary epic/progressive particle 'continually / ever'. نوش is a noun 'delight / pleasant drink' used metaphorically here as 'pleasure'. جست is the past of جستن in the sense 'sought' (sense 1). از جهان is 'from the world / from life' (جهان as 'this life, existence') and یافت is 'he found'. زهر is the resultative noun 'poison' — together: 'ever seeking pleasure from the world, he found poison.' The clause uses the noun+verb (نوش جست) pattern common in classical Persian where a noun of pleasure is paired with جستن 'to seek'.
segments.s129-g1: فرستاده شد is a passive/perfect construction: فرستاده (past participle or 'sent') + شد (auxiliary 'be') = 'was sent'. نزد قیصر is a prepositional phrase 'to/at Caesar's presence' (نزد used as 'to/in the presence of'). ز شاه uses the archaic poetic preposition ز (= از) to indicate source/agent: 'from the king'. Word order in classical Persian places the passive predicate first and the source/recipient phrases after it.
segments.s129-g2: که introduces a relative clause modifying سواری ('a horseman'). اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/along' placed before the verb. نوردید is a perfective past meaning 'traversed' or 'rode over' (here describing completed motion). راه is the object 'road'; the clause literally reads 'who in/along traversed the road.'
segments.s129-g3: بفرمود is a courtly/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (introducing a reported order). کز here is the contracted classical form meaning 'from among' (essentially از + که). نامداران روم is a genitival phrase 'the renowned ones of Rome' serving as the source set for the selection.
segments.s129-g4: کسی is an indefinite 'someone'. کاو is the archaic relative 'که او' ('who/that he') — this usage is not in the provided senses list (so marked -1). بنازد is a present/formal verb meaning 'takes pride in' or 'is proud of' (here describing the quality required of that someone). بران = 'upon/that' (thereon), مرز و بوم = 'border and homeland'. The clause thus says 'someone who takes pride in that border and homeland.'
segments.s129-g5: جهان‌دیده is a compound adjective meaning 'world-seen' (worldly, experienced). باید here functions as a modal/lexical verb conveying necessity ('needs' / 'must have'). عنان‌دار کس is a noun phrase 'a person who holds the reins' (عنان‌دار = 'reins-bearing', کس = 'person/someone'); note the adjective-like عنان‌دار precedes the noun in this inverted classical order.
segments.s129-g6: سنان و سپر is a coordinated pair 'spear and shield' (objects of need). بایدش is باید plus the clitic ش ('for him'/'he needs it'), so the verb indicates necessity directed toward that person. یار means 'companion/ally' and بس ('enough') qualifies it: the line says he needs companions (for spear and shield), enough/adequate allies. Word order places the clitic on the verb (بایدش) as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s129-g7: چنین modifies لشکری: 'such an army/force'. باید again expresses necessity ('is needed' / 'must be'). از مرز روم is a prepositional phrase 'from the Roman border/frontier' specifying the source of that force. This is a standard S (modifier) + N + predicate + prepositional-phrase order in epic Persian.
segments.s129-g8: که introduces a relative clause qualifying لشکری ('an army that...'). آیند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive ('they come' / 'will come') used here to mean 'that will come.' با من 'with me' and به آباد بوم 'to the prosperous/habited homeland' are consecutive prepositional phrases indicating accompaniment and destination. The clause describes the desired army as one that will accompany the speaker to the inhabited land.
segments.s129-g73: This is a compact prepositional clause giving origin and motion: ز (prepositional/poetic 'from') + بربر (agent/source) + بیامد (past tense 'came') + سوی (preposition 'toward') + سوریان (object 'the Syrians'). Word order is source → verb → goal, typical of older/poetic Persian where the verb immediately follows the subject/agent phrase.
segments.s129-g74: This noun phrase names the arriving force: یکی ('one' / 'a certain') modifies لشکری ('an army'), followed by the adjectives بی‌کران ('boundless, innumerable') and میان ('middle / midst' used here as a terse qualifying element). In classical Persian such adjective/noun clusters often appear after the head noun; here the sense is 'a single, vast army'.
segments.s129-g75: This clause has a dative/benefactive phrase followed by a past verb: به ('for/to') + جنگش ('his/that battle') + بیاراست (classical past, 'arrayed, prepared') + افراسیاب (subject). The verb بیاراست is an archaic/poetic past meaning 'arrayed/put in order', and the possessor suffix in جنگش indicates 'his/their battle'.
segments.s129-g76: Poetic verbless/semi-verbal clause describing the scene: به گردون ('to/into the sky/heavens') + همی (an older adverbial particle marking ongoing or emphatic action) + خاک ('dust') + برزد (archaic verb; here 'leapt up/was cast/rose') + ز ('from') + آب ('water'). The subject is implied (dust) and برزد is an archaic intransitive verb meaning movement upward/outward in this context.
segments.s129-g77: Causal clause: جهان ('the world') + کر شد (compound past 'became deaf') + از ('from / because of') + ناله ('wailing/moan') + بوق و کوس ('trumpet and war‑drum'). شد is the past of شدن used to form 'became', and از introduces the cause (the loud sounds of instruments).
segments.s129-g78: Parallel nominal-predicate clauses with شد linking subject to predicate: زمین ('the earth') + آهنین ('iron/steely') + شد ('became'); followed by a coordinate clause هوا ('the air/sky') + آبنوس ('ebony, very dark/black') with the same implied verb. This is a poetic use of 'شد' to assert dramatic transformation.
segments.s129-g79: This is a paired prepositional cause phrase: ز ('from') + زخم ('wound') + تبرزین ('battle-axe') gives 'from the wounds of the axe', then و ('and') + از ('from') + بس ('so much / such an abundance') + ترنگ ('hue/color'). Together they enumerate sources/reasons for what follows. بس here has an emphatic/quantifying sense ('so much, such').
segments.s129-g80: Main result clause with an archaic/progressive particle: همی (progressive/emphatic) + موج ('a wave') + خون ('blood') + خاست (past/archaic 'arose, sprang up') + از ('from') + دشت ('plain/field') + جنگ ('battle'). The verb خاست is an older past form meaning 'arose', and دشت جنگ is the locative 'field of battle'.
segments.s129-g49: ندانی is the negative present/imperative-stem form meaning "you do not know" (ن + دانی). که is the subordinating conjunction "that" introducing the clause. ایران is the place-name "Iran." نشست is used here in the sense of "is settled/stands" (a stative verb/noun sense), and من‌ست is a predicative phrase "belongs to me" (من + است, contracted poetically), so the clause reads literally "you do not know that Iran is settled/belongs to me."
segments.s129-g50: جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic phrase meaning "the whole world, entirely" (سر به سر = all/everywhere). زیر دست is a prepositional phrase "under the hand/of the authority of," with دست here meaning "power/control." من‌ست again is the predicative "is mine/belongs to me." Word order puts the emphatic "جهان سر به سر" up front, then the locative/possessive predicate "زیر دست من‌ست."
segments.s129-g51: پلنگ ژیان is a nominal phrase "the leopard (of life/wild)" (ژیان is an epithet), and گرچه ... باشد forms a concessive clause "although it may be/though it is." باشد is the present/subjunctive form of "to be" used here as "may be/is," and دلیر is the predicate adjective "brave." The construction expresses: "Though the leopard may be brave..."
segments.s129-g52: This clause gives the outcome: شدن is the verbal infinitive/auxiliary "to become / to be (made)" (here forming a passive sense), پیش = "before / in front of / in the presence of," چنگال شیر = "the lion's claw(s)." The first token نیارد is archaic/uncertain in meaning in the supplied senses (it does not match the listed senses), so it is treated as negating/preventing escape in context; overall: "it does not avoid/become in front of the lion's claw" → "it does not escape being seized by the lion's claws."
segments.s129-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction "when." آگاهی here means "news / information." آمد is the past tense "came" and به marks the indirect object "to Afrasiab." The phrase is a straightforward temporal clause: "When the news came to Afrasiab..."
segments.s129-g54: سرش is the possessive compound "his head" (سر + ـش enclitic = his). پر ز کین گشت is a causative/stative sequence: پر = "full (of)," ز = the poetic preposition "from / with / because of," کین = "vengeance/hatred," and گشت = "became." So "his head became full of hatred." Then و links the next clause: دل پرشتاب = "his heart (was) full of haste/was hurried," where پرشتاب is an adjective "full of haste/perturbed."
segments.s129-g55: فرستاد is the simple past "he sent." پاسخش ="his reply" (پاسخ + enclitic ش = his). کاین is a demonstrative/relative "that which / this that" introducing the content of the reply, and گفت‌وگوی is the construct form of "conversation / talk." Together: "He sent his reply that this conversation..."
segments.s129-g56: نزیبد is the negated/poetic form of زیبد (from زیبیدن) meaning "(it) befits / is fitting," so نزیبد = "it is not fitting/it does not become." جز از = "except from/except for." مردم زشت خوی is a genitive phrase "people of ugly/vile disposition" (زشت خوی = ill-natured). The predicate is negative and the exception phrase follows: "Such talk is not fitting except for vile-natured people."
segments.s129-g17: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” از گرگساران is an ablative phrase “from/out of the wolf-haunts” (گرگساران here = wolfish people or brigand-country); the verb بیامد (past) follows the locative phrase and agrees with the subject سپاه (an army/host) which appears after the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s129-g18: که introduces a relative/circumstantial clause referring back to سپاه (“an army that …”). جویند is the present/plural classical form “they seek.” گاه here means a place/time/occasion (i.e. the whereabouts or opportunity), and سرافراز شاه is the noun phrase “the triumphant/lofty king” which is what they are seeking.
segments.s129-g19: دل ما is the subject “our heart/feeling.” شد is the past auxiliary/verb “became,” and the post-positional phrase از کار ایشان gives the cause (“because of their deeds”). بدرد here means “to ache / be hurt,” so شد ... بدرد = “became pained.”
segments.s129-g20: که is causal here (“for / because”). دلشان is a possessed noun “their heart(s).” چنین modifies the following noun (so: “thus/so”), برتری is “superiority,” and یاد کرد is the compound verb ‘یاد کردن’ meaning “to remember/recollect” (کرد is the past auxiliary). The clause explains the reason their hearts pained the speaker.
segments.s129-g21: همی is a classical/poetic adverb marking continuity or emphasis (here “still/indeed”). تاج او is the object “his crown” placed before the verb in this older word order; خواست is the past tense “desired/wanted,” and افراسیاب is the subject (name) placed after the verb—typical of Ferdowsi’s poetic inversion.
segments.s129-g22: ز راه خرد literally “from/by the way of wisdom.” Here the phrase signals departure from prudent counsel or the context of wisdom. سرش is “his head” (ش = his), گشته is a perfect/participial or resultive verb meaning “has become/turned,” and شتاب is the noun “haste/hastefulness”; together the clause means his head became hurried/impatient.
segments.s129-g23: برفتیم is the past first‑person plural “we went/advanced.” با + نیزه‌های دراز is the instrumental phrase “with/using long spears”; نیزه‌های is the plural possessive form of spear and دراز is the following adjective “long.”
segments.s129-g24: برو here functions as a connective/poetic particle (roughly “thereupon/then” or a colloquial linking ‘and so’) introducing the result clause. تلخ کردیم is “we made [it] bitter” (کردیم = we made/did), and آرام و ناز are the direct objects “calm/peace and pride/arrogance” that are turned to bitterness. The clause says the speakers spoiled their tranquillity and pride.
segments.s129-g41: که here introduces a consequence/result clause ('so/therefore'); ایران بپرداز is an imperative/command-like clause where بپرداز (classical form of پرداختن) tells someone to take/attend to Iran. The negative prohibition مجوی follows with بیشی ("more/excess") as its object: literal word order is object (فزونی/بیشی) before verb (مجوی), a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s129-g42: This clause gives the result/reason: سر ما شد is a predicate where سر (metaphorical 'position/standing') is the subject and شد (became) is the verb; از تو marks cause ('because of you'); پر از گفت‌وگوی is a adjectival phrase meaning 'full of discussion/dispute' with پر (full) + از + noun. Word order is Subject–Object–Verb typical of classical Persian phrasing.
segments.s129-g43: ترا here functions as the indirect object 'to you' (written as the clitic ت + object marker را). شهر توران is the direct object ('the city of Turan'), and بسندست خود is an attributive phrase 'with a sure/steady hand of my/your own' (بسندست = 'with a sure hand', خود = 'own'). The whole phrase therefore means 'I give/put the city of Turan into your own sure hand.'
segments.s129-g44: به خیره here is an adverbial phrase 'in astonishment/with eyes staring' (به + adjective used adverbially). همی is the classical continuous/iterative particle ("still/ever"), and دست یازی is a compound meaning 'to lay hands on / seize / reach forth a hand'; ببد is an archaic/copular past-form (classical variant of شدن/بودن) rendering the clause in narrative past. Together: 'you will, astonished, reach/lay your hand (upon it).'
segments.s129-g45: فزونی مجوی is a prohibitive imperative: فزونی ('increase/more') is the thing not to be sought, followed by مجوی ('do not seek') — noun before verb is a normal classical order. ار شدی بی‌نیاز is a conditional 'if you become self-sufficient' (ار = if, شدی = you become, بی‌نیاز = free from need).
segments.s129-g46: که introduces the explanatory/result clause 'for/that': درد آردت is 'it will bring you pain' with آرد (classical subjunctive/present of آوردن used narratively) + clitic ت 'you' as object; پیش رنج دراز is a prepositional phrase 'before/into long suffering' (پیش = before/in the face of, رنج دراز = prolonged suffering). The clause gives the bad consequence of seeking more.
segments.s129-g47: ترا (ت + را) marks the addressee ('for you'); کهتری کار بستن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase 'to take/settle a lesser job/role' (کهتری = lesser, کار بستن = to conclude/accept a task); نکوست is the predicate 'is good/fitting.' The structure expresses moral advice: accepting a lesser role is advisable.
segments.s129-g48: نگه‌داشتن is an infinitive 'to keep/preserve'; بر تن خویش پوست is a literal expression 'the skin on one's body' (بر = on/upon, تن = body, خوی + ش = 'one's own' formed by noun + possessive clitic, پوست = skin). Together this idiom means 'to preserve one's life/keep one's skin on one's body' — i.e., to survive.
segments.s129-g81: This is a compressed poetic noun phrase naming a person: a chain of nouns without the expected ezāfe. سر (here 'head/chief') + بخت ('fortune') + گردان ('troop') together form an epithet governing the name افراسیاب; taken together it means something like 'the fortunate/chief of the troop' (i.e. Afrasiab as leader of a fortunate troop). Such noun stacks are common in classical poetry.
segments.s129-g82: بران is an adverbial/directional particle 'to/onto that (place)'; رزم‌گاه means 'battlefield'; اندر 'into/in' is an archaic preposition; آمد is the past tense 'he came'. بخواب is a verbal noun 'to sleep' used with آمد to indicate the ensuing action ('came to sleep' / 'came and lay down to sleep'). Word order is poetic: directional phrase + place + motion verb + verbal noun.
segments.s129-g83: دو بهره here gives a numerical subject ('two portions/two of them'); ز marks origin ('from Turan'); سپه 'army/host' is the source; کشته شد is a passive construction ('were killed') formed by the past participle کشته + auxiliary شد. The overall structure is 'two [units] from Turan's army were slain.'
segments.s129-g84: سرسرکشان is the subject 'the proud ones' or 'proud leaders'; پاک here functions adverbially ('completely/cleanly', idiomatically 'unharmed/without loss'); برگشته is the past participle 'returned'; شد again is the auxiliary forming the result ('have returned'). The order places the modifier پاک before the participle for emphasis.
segments.s129-g85: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. چون ... دید is a temporal/causal clause 'when/when he saw' (چون here introduces the time or circumstance); کار 'the matter/affair'; زان‌گونه 'of that sort/so' refers back to the preceding situation. Together: 'When the commander saw the affair was so...' Note the classical ordering: subject — conjunction — object — verb.
segments.s129-g86: بی‌آتش literally 'without fire' (an adverbial/qualifier). بجوشید is the past tense 'he boiled/seethed' (here figurative, emotional agitation). همچون introduces a simile 'like/as', and نبید is 'wine' — so the clause says he seethed (with passion) though without blaze, like wine seething. The verb is intransitive past with the adverbial preceding it for emphasis.
segments.s129-g87: به آواز گفت is a manner phrase: به + آواز ('with/by voice') + گفت (past of گفتن 'he said'), so 'he said aloud'. ای دلیران من is a vocative: ای 'O', دلیران 'brave ones', من follows the noun as a possessive ('my') in classical Persian word order — 'O my brave ones.'
segments.s129-g88: This is a string of epithets addressing the troops: گزیده 'chosen/elite' modifies یلان 'heroes'; نره شیران is a compound epithet 'manly lions' (نره 'manly/virile' qualifying شیران 'lions' used metaphorically for brave men); من again is the possessive 'my'. Such piled epithets are typical in epic vocatives.
segments.s129-g89: شما را is the personal pronoun 'you' followed by the object marker را, marking 'you' as the direct object. ز بهر is an archaic/prepositional pair meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of' (ز here used poetically). چنین روزگار is a noun phrase 'such a time/times' modifying the purpose expressed by ز بهر.
segments.s129-g90: همی is a classical/poetic adverb indicating continuous or habitual action ('always, continually'). پرورانیدم is 1st person singular past/imperfective 'I reared/fostered' (classical form, -دم ending). اندر کنار is a locative phrase meaning 'at (my) side / in the company of', completing the predicate and linking back to the object شما را.
segments.s129-g91: بکوشید is a 2nd-person plural exhortative/imperfect form ('strive' / 'make an effort'), used here as a command. و هم links the next action ('and also'); پشت is a noun 'rear/behind' (i.e., the rear of the fighting) and آورید is 2nd-person plural imperative 'bring' (classical/plural ending -ید). The clause therefore commands: 'also bring the rear (support) into the battle.'
segments.s129-g92: جهان را places 'the world' as a definite object with the object marker را. به کاووس marks the beneficiary/target 'for/unto Kavus.' تنگ آورید is a causative construction: تنگ 'narrow' + آورید ('cause/bring about') — literally 'cause (the world) to be narrow for Kavus,' idiomatically 'make the world close/tight for Kavus' or 'make the world submit/constrict for Kavus.'
segments.s129-g93: یلان را is the definite object ('the warriors/heroes') marked by را. به before ژوپین و خنجر here has an instrumental meaning ('by/with') in poetic usage. ژوپین (gambeson) و خنجر (dagger) are instruments, and زنید is a 2nd-person plural imperative 'strike/attack/kill' (classical plural ending -ید).
segments.s129-g94: دلیرانشان 'their brave ones' is a noun with a possessive suffix (their). سر به سر is an idiomatic repetitive phrase meaning 'one and all' or 'head to head/one by one' (lit. 'head to head'). بفگنید is an imperative (2pl) meaning 'scatter/put to rout/destroy' in this context; together the clause orders: 'put their champions to rout, one and all.'
segments.s129-g95: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same.' سگزی is an epithet (poetic / archaic adjective) attached before the name; here treated as a descriptive epithet. رستم is the proper name and شیردل is a postpositive epithet 'lion‑hearted' (classical Persian commonly stacks epithets after names).
segments.s129-g96: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. از شیر literally 'from (a) lion' (از as 'from/of'), here metaphorically 'from the lion (i.e. lion's courage)'. بستد is a classical 3rd-person singular past form 'bound/tied' (archaic spelling with -د). به شمشیر دل is a dative/instrumental phrase 'to/with the sword of (his) heart' — together: 'who, from the lion, bound (his) heart to the sword.'
segments.s129-g65: به here is the instrumental/prepositional particle 'with' (classical use). شمشیر is the instrument noun 'sword'. بستانم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/optative form ('I take' / 'I will take' / 'let me take') typical in epic diction. از + کوه marks the source 'from the mountain' with تیغ as the noun 'blade' (note: the modifier عقاب that specifies whose blade is split off and appears in the next segment for metrical/poetic reasons).
segments.s129-g66: تیغ (blade) is completed by the following noun عقاب ('the eagle's blade'); اندر is the locative 'in/within'. آرم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will rest / I will place / I will set' (continuing the speaker's action). ز introduces a source/relation ('from'), تاریک modifies میغ as 'dark blade' — the phrase as a whole expresses where/how the speaker will lay or place the blade.
segments.s129-g67: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. آمدم is first‑person perfect/past 'I came' (here used in narration: 'I have come' / 'I come'). جنگ را ساخته uses ساخته (past participle 'made/prepared') with را marking جنگ as the definite/accused object — together 'having prepared the war'. This participial construction is a compact classical way to express 'having prepared the battle'.
segments.s129-g68: درفش is 'banner/standard' and درفشان here is adjectival/appositive 'shining, glittering'. برافراخته is a past participle used predicatively ('raised up / hoisted'). The noun + adjective + participle order is typical in Persian epic: 'the shining standard (is) raised aloft.'
segments.s129-g69: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. برگشت is the simple past 'returned'. مانند + باد is a simile 'like the wind' (باد here is literal 'wind', not the optative particle). The phrase describes swift return.
segments.s129-g70: سخنها is the plural object 'the words/news/messages'. به کاووس is the indirect object 'to Kavus'. کی is the interrogative adverb 'when' placed before the verb, and کرد is the simple past 'did/made' used here in the light‑verb construction کرد + یاد ('to make a remembrance' = 'to mention/relate'). یاد is the noun 'mention/remembrance' so the clause asks when he related/told the matter to Kavus.
segments.s129-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is third‑person past 'he heard'. گفتار اوی is the noun 'his speech/utterance' with اوی as the possessive 'his' (classical enclitic form). The clause sets the time/condition for the following action.
segments.s129-g72: بیاراست is a past/preterite verb 'he adorned/arrayed/equipped' (here third person, understood subject 'he' = Kāvus). لشکر is the direct object 'army'. به پیکار is a prepositional phrase 'for battle' (purpose), and اوی is the possessive 'his' modifying لشکر. Altogether: 'he arrayed his army for battle.'
segments.s129-g97: بود is the past copula 'was' (third-person) introducing a characterizing clause. کز is the conjunction 'که/از که' meaning 'that / because (of)', linking the characterization to the reason. دلیری is the abstract noun 'bravery'; ببند آورید is a compound verbal idea: ببند (root 'to bind/close') combined with آورید (classical 3rd‑person plural/past-form of آورد or an optative) yields 'they would bind/ensnare / cause to be bound' — here expressing that others, on account of his boldness, would contrive to bind or ensnare him.
segments.s129-g98: سرش را is the direct object phrase: سر 'head' + the enclitic ش marking possession ('his' here) and then را the definite/object marker. به دام is a prepositional phrase 'into/into the trap' (به = to/into, دام = trap). گزند آورید is another compound where گزند 'harm' is combined with آورید 'they brought/caused' — together 'they brought harm (upon him)' or 'they inflicted harm', with آورید functioning as the verb taking گزند as the notion of damage.
segments.s129-g99: هرآنکس 'whoever' begins a relative clause introduced by که 'that/who'. او را is the object 'him' with را as the definite object marker. به‌روز literally 'by day / in daylight' here means 'in open battle'. نبرد is the classical negative past/auxiliary form (ن + برد) meaning 'did not fight/meet' — so the clause reads 'whoever did not fight him in open combat.'
segments.s129-g100: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. زین 'saddle' + پلنگ 'leopard' is a descriptive compound 'leopard-saddle' (a poetic epithet for a saddle or war-harness). اندر آرد is a verbal phrase (آرد = 'brings' / 'takes') with اندر (in/into) giving the sense 'bring/take into'. به گرد is a prepositional phrase: به 'to/into' + گرد 'around/into the circle' (here 'into the ring/assembly/round'), so the whole segment means 'they would take him from the leopard-saddle and bring him into the circle/arena.'
segments.s129-g101: دهم is the first‑person form 'I give' (classical present/optative). دختر خویش is 'my daughter' (خوی + ش form the reflexive/possessive 'خویش' = 'one's own'; here 'my' by agreement with دهم). و شاهی ورا links 'and the kingship' (شاهی = kingship) with ورا 'to him/thither' as the indirect object: 'I give him my daughter and the kingdom.'
segments.s129-g102: برآرم is first‑person 'I will lift/raise' (بر + آرم; classical 1sg). سر 'head' is the direct object; از 'from' introduces origin/place; برج 'tower' and ماهی 'fish' together likely form a place-name or epithet 'Tower of Mahi' (ماهی can also be a proper/poetic name). ورا again marks direction 'to him/thither'. The clause thus promises to 'raise his head from the Tower of Mahi' (i.e., exalt or free him from that place).
segments.s129-g103: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. شنیدند is the past plural 'they heard'. گفتار is the noun 'speech/words', and اوی is the old/poetic possessive form 'his' (variant of او/اش), so 'when the Turks heard his words.'
segments.s129-g104: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout / all (together)'. سوی رزم is the prepositional phrase 'toward battle' (سوی = toward, رزم = battle). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' used here with روی as its object to mean 'they turned (their) faces'. روی is the noun 'face'; together the phrase means 'they all turned (their) faces) toward battle' — i.e., they prepared for combat.

sentences.s129-s5: Then news came from the level plains; to the plain came horsemen and spearmen.
sentences.s129-s6: — telling what Rostam had done in Egypt and among the Berbers, and what he did to those kings on the day of battle.
sentences.s129-s7: They sought bravery; horsemen rode about — rein‑twisters, man‑slayers and spear‑bearers.
sentences.s129-s8: They wrote a letter — a manly one — with fitting and rich words.
sentences.s129-s29: If you were worthy of Iran, you would not have needed Mazandaran.
sentences.s129-s30: He said thus: 'Iran is double‑faced toward me; one ought to hear honest words.'
sentences.s129-s31: For he is a son of Fereydun, my ancestor; all the towns of Iran are my dwelling.
sentences.s129-s32: And furthermore, by the arm of the swordsman I emptied the assembly of the Arabs.
sentences.s129-s17: We will drench the land from mountain to mountain in blood; we will make the enemy’s plain like the Jihun (Amu Darya).
sentences.s129-s18: He threw off the Arab envoy and left; he turned his face toward Barbarestan and scorched (it).
sentences.s129-s19: When the messenger reached the Shah of Iran, he found that such words were fitting.
sentences.s129-s20: He liked them and put the plan into effect; at that time he sent a letter to Afrasiab.
sentences.s129-s13: Many were slain on both sides; the times turned to every kind of good and ill.
sentences.s129-s14: Now news came of his deeds: that the imperial throne had been renewed.
sentences.s129-s15: All the renowned swordsmen, at that place of hatred, rose and formed an assembly.
sentences.s129-s16: When the king yields the reins to a barbarian, we will thrust a spear clean through his neck.
sentences.s129-s53: He rushed swiftly with the Syrian host; in seeking that gain the outcome was loss.
sentences.s129-s54: When he saw the state of affairs so clearly, from there he drew toward the city of Turan.
sentences.s129-s55: His heart was weary and the two hosts lay slain; ever seeking delight in the world, he found poison.
sentences.s129-s1: From the king a messenger was sent to Caesar — a horseman who had traversed the road.
sentences.s129-s2: He commanded that from among Rome's famed men someone who would take pride in that frontier and homeland (be chosen).
sentences.s129-s3: A man experienced in the world needs a reins-holder; for spear and shield he needs companions enough.
sentences.s129-s4: Such a force is needed from the Roman frontier — one that will come with me to the prosperous homeland.
sentences.s129-s37: From the barbarians there came toward the Syrians one vast, innumerable army.
sentences.s129-s38: Afrasiab arrayed himself for battle; to the heavens even dust leapt up from the water.
sentences.s129-s39: The world was made deaf by the wailing of trumpet and drum; the earth became iron and the air ebony.
sentences.s129-s40: From the axe-wounds and from the great profusion of color, a wave of blood rose from the field of battle.
sentences.s129-s25: Do you not know that Iran is mine? The whole world, utterly, lies under my hand.
sentences.s129-s26: Although the wild leopard may be brave, it does not escape being laid before the lion's claw.
sentences.s129-s27: When the news came to Afrasiab, his head filled with hatred and his heart with haste.
sentences.s129-s28: He sent his reply that such a conversation befitted no one except people of ugly disposition.
sentences.s129-s9: When an army came out of the wolf-haunts, seeking the triumphant king's place.
sentences.s129-s10: Our heart was pained by their deeds, for their hearts thus recalled their superiority.
sentences.s129-s11: Afrasiab still desired his crown; abandoning the path of wisdom, his head was seized by haste.
sentences.s129-s12: We advanced with long spears, and so made their calm and pride bitter.
sentences.s129-s21: Take (hold of) Iran and seek no more; because of you our position has become full of argument.
sentences.s129-s22: I will put the city of Turan into your own sure hand; you will, astonished, lay hands on it.
sentences.s129-s23: Seek no increase; if you become self-sufficient, (remember) that it will bring you pain and long suffering.
sentences.s129-s24: It is good for you to take on a lesser post; to keep your skin upon your body is (what matters).
sentences.s129-s41: Afrasiab, leader of the fortunate troop, came into that battlefield and lay down to sleep.
sentences.s129-s42: Two portions of Turan's host were slain; the haughty chiefs returned unharmed.
sentences.s129-s43: When the commander saw that the affair was thus, he boiled without flame like wine.
sentences.s129-s44: He cried aloud: 'O my brave men, my chosen heroes, my manly lions!'
sentences.s129-s45: I raised you for such times, always at my side.
sentences.s129-s46: Strive, and likewise bring up the rear in battle; make the world narrow for Kavus.
sentences.s129-s47: Strike the warriors with gambesons and daggers; put their champions to rout one and all.
sentences.s129-s48: That same Sagzi Rostam, lion‑hearted, who drew his courage from the lion and bound it to his sword.
sentences.s129-s33: With my sword I will take from the mountain the eagle's blade; I will set it within the dark blade.
sentences.s129-s34: Now I have come, having prepared the war; the shining banner I have raised aloft.
sentences.s129-s35: The messenger returned like the wind; when did he bring the news to Kavus?
sentences.s129-s36: When Kavus heard his words, he arrayed his army for battle.
sentences.s129-s49: He was such that, because of his bravery, they would ensnare him and bring harm upon his head.
sentences.s129-s50: Whoever did not face him in open battle by day, they would drag him from the leopard‑saddle and bring him into the ring.
sentences.s129-s51: I will give him my daughter and the kingship, and will raise his head from the Tower of Mahi.
sentences.s129-s52: When the Turks heard his speech, they all turned their faces toward battle.
